1.1: All wisdom comes from the LORD and with him it remains forever.
1.2: The sand of the seashore, the drops of rain, the days of eternity: who can number these?
1.3: Heaven's height, earth's breadth, the depths of the abyss: who can explore these?
1.4: Before all things else wisdom was created; and prudent understanding, from eternity.
1.5: To whom has wisdom's root been revealed? Who knows her subtleties?
1.6: There is but one, wise and truly awe-inspiring, seated upon his throne:
1.7: It is the LORD; he created her, has seen her and taken note of her.
1.8: He has poured her forth upon all his works, upon every living thing according to his bounty; he has lavished her upon his friends.
1.9: Fear of the LORD is glory and splendor, gladness and a festive crown.
1.10: Fear of the LORD warms the heart, giving gladness and joy and length of days.
1.11: He who fears the LORD will have a happy end; even on the day of his death he will be blessed.
1.12: The beginning of wisdom is fear of the LORD, which is formed with the faithful in the womb.
1.13: With devoted men was she created from of old, and with their children her beneficence abides.
1.14: Fullness of wisdom is fear of the LORD; she inebriates men with her fruits.
1.15: Her entire house she fills with choice foods, her granaries with her harvest.
1.16: Wisdom's garland is fear of the LORD, with blossoms of peace and perfect health.
1.17: Knowledge and full understanding she showers down; she heightens the glory of those who possess her.
1.18: The root of wisdom is fear of the LORD; her branches are length of days.
1.19: One cannot justify unjust anger; anger plunges a man to his downfall.
1.20: A patient man need stand firm but for a time, and then contentment comes back to him.
1.21: For a while he holds back his words, then the lips of many herald his wisdom.
1.22: Among wisdom's treasures is the paragon of prudence; but fear of the LORD is an abomination to the sinner.
1.23: If you desire wisdom, keep the commandments, and the LORD will bestow her upon you;
1.24: For fear of the LORD is wisdom and culture; loyal humility is his delight.
1.25: Be not faithless to the fear of the LORD, nor approach it with duplicity of heart.
1.26: Play not the hypocrite before men; over your lips keep watch.
1.27: Exalt not yourself lest you fall and bring upon you dishonor;
1.28: For then the LORD will reveal your secrets and publicly cast you down,
1.29: Because you approached the fear of the LORD with your heart full of guile.

2.1: My son, when you come to serve the LORD, prepare yourself for trials.
2.2: Be sincere of heart and steadfast, undisturbed in time of adversity.
2.3: Cling to him, forsake him not; thus will your future be great.
2.4: Accept whatever befalls you, in crushing misfortune be patient;
2.5: For in fire gold is tested, and worthy men in the crucible of humiliation.
2.6: Trust God and he will help you; make straight your ways and hope in him.
2.7: You who fear the LORD, wait for his mercy, turn not away lest you fall.
2.8: You who fear the LORD, trust him, and your reward will not be lost.
2.9: You who fear the LORD, hope for good things, for lasting joy and mercy.
2.10: Study the generations long past and understand; has anyone hoped in the LORD and been disappointed? Has anyone persevered in his fear and been forsaken? has anyone called upon him and been rebuffed?
2.11: Compassionate and merciful is the LORD; he forgives sins, he saves in time of trouble.
2.12: Woe to craven hearts and drooping hands, to the sinner who treads a double path!
2.13: Woe to the faint of heart who trust not, who therefore will have no shelter!
2.14: Woe to you who have lost hope! what will you do at the visitation of the LORD?
2.15: Those who fear the LORD disobey not his words; those who love him keep his ways.
2.16: Those who fear the LORD seek to please him, those who love him are filled with his law.
2.17: Those who fear the LORD prepare their hearts and humble themselves before him.
2.18: Let us fall into the hands of the LORD and not into the hands of men, for equal to his majesty is the mercy that he shows.

3.1: Children, pay heed to a father's right; do so that you may live.
3.2: For the LORD sets a father in honor over his children; a mother's authority he confirms over her sons.
3.3: He who honors his father atones for sins;
3.4: he stores up riches who reveres his mother.
3.5: He who honors his father is gladdened by children, and when he prays he is heard.
3.6: He who reveres his father will live a long life; he obeys the LORD who brings comfort to his mother.
3.7: He who fears the LORD honors his father, and serves his parents as rulers.
3.8: In word and deed honor your father that his blessing may come upon you;
3.9: For a father's blessing gives a family firm roots, but a mother's curse uproots the growing plant.
3.10: Glory not in your father's shame, for his shame is no glory to you!
3.11: His father's honor is a man's glory; disgrace for her children, a mother's shame.
3.12: My son, take care of your father when he is old; grieve him not as long as he lives.
3.13: Even if his mind fail, be considerate with him; revile him not in the fullness of your strength.
3.14: For kindness to a father will not be forgotten, it will serve as a sin offering—it will take lasting root.
3.15: In time of tribulation it will be recalled to your advantage, like warmth upon frost it will melt away your sins.
3.16: A blasphemer is he who despises his father; accursed of his Creator, he who angers his mother.
3.17: My son, conduct your affairs with humility, and you will be loved more than a giver of gifts.
3.18: Humble yourself the more, the greater you are, and you will find favor with God.
3.19: For great is the power of God; by the humble he is glorified.
3.20: What is too sublime for you, seek not, into things beyond your strength search not.
3.21: What is committed to you, attend to; for what is hidden is not your concern.
3.22: With what is too much for you meddle not, when shown things beyond human understanding.
3.23: Their own opinion has misled many, and false reasoning unbalanced their judgment.
3.24: Where the pupil of the eye is missing, there is no light, and where there is no knowledge, there is no wisdom.
3.25: A stubborn man will fare badly in the end, and he who loves danger will perish in it.
3.26: A stubborn man will be burdened with sorrow; a sinner will heap sin upon sin.
3.27: For the affliction of the proud man there is no cure; he is the offshoot of an evil plant.
3.28: The mind of a sage appreciates proverbs, and an attentive ear is the wise man's joy.
3.29: Water quenches a flaming fire, and alms atone for sins.
3.30: He who does a kindness is remembered afterward; when he falls, he finds a support.

4.1: My son, rob not the poor man of his livelihood; force not the eyes of the needy to turn away.
4.2: A hungry man grieve not, a needy man anger not;
4.3: Do not exasperate the downtrodden; delay not to give to the needy.
4.4: A beggar in distress do not reject; avert not your face from the poor.
4.5: From the needy turn not your eyes, give no man reason to curse you;
4.6: For if in the bitterness of his soul he curse you, his Creator will hear his prayer.
4.7: Endear yourself to the assembly; before a ruler bow your head.
4.8: Give a hearing to the poor man, and return his greeting with courtesy;
4.9: Deliver the oppressed from the hand of the oppressor; let not justice be repugnant to you.
4.10: To the fatherless be as a father, and help their mother as a husband would; Thus will you be like a son to the Most High, and he will be more tender to you than a mother.
4.11: Wisdom instructs her children and admonishes those who seek her.
4.12: He who loves her loves life; those who seek her out win her favor.
4.13: He who holds her fast inherits glory; wherever he dwells, the LORD bestows blessings.
4.14: Those who serve her serve the Holy One; those who love her the LORD loves.
4.15: He who obeys her judges nations; he who hearkens to her dwells in her inmost chambers.
4.16: If one trusts her, he will possess her; his descendants too will inherit her.
4.17: She walks with him as a stranger, and at first she puts him to the test; Fear and dread she brings upon him and tries him with her discipline; With her precepts she puts him to the proof, until his heart is fully with her.
4.18: Then she comes back to bring him happiness and reveal her secrets to him.
4.19: But if he fails her, she will abandon him and deliver him into the hands of despoilers.
4.20: Use your time well; guard yourself from evil, and bring upon yourself no shame.
4.21: There is a sense of shame laden with guilt, and a shame that merits honor and respect.
4.22: Show no favoritism to your own discredit; let no one intimidate you to your own downfall.
4.23: Refrain not from speaking at the proper time, and hide not away your wisdom;
4.24: For it is through speech that wisdom becomes known, and knowledge through the tongue's rejoinder.
4.25: Never gainsay the truth, and struggle not against the rushing stream.
4.26: Be not ashamed to acknowledge your guilt, but of your ignorance rather be ashamed.
4.27: Do not abase yourself before an impious man, nor refuse to do so before rulers.
4.28: Even to the death fight for truth, and the LORD your God will battle for you.
4.29: Be not surly in your speech, nor lazy and slack in your deeds.
4.30: Be not a lion at home, nor sly and suspicious at work.
4.31: Let not your hand be open to receive and clenched when it is time to give.

5.1: Rely not on your wealth; say not: "I have the power."
5.2: Rely not on your strength in following the desires of your heart.
5.3: Say not: "Who can prevail against me?" for the LORD will exact the punishment.
5.4: Say not: "I have sinned, yet what has befallen me?" for the LORD bides his time.
5.5: Of forgiveness be not overconfident, adding sin upon sin.
5.6: Say not: "Great is his mercy; my many sins he will forgive."
5.7: For mercy and anger alike are with him; upon the wicked alights his wrath.
5.8: Delay not your conversion to the LORD, put it not off from day to day;
5.9: For suddenly his wrath flames forth; at the time of vengeance, you will be destroyed.
5.10: Rely not upon deceitful wealth, for it will be no help on the day of wrath.
5.11: Winnow not in every wind, and start not off in every direction.
5.12: Be consistent in your thoughts; steadfast be your words.
5.13: Be swift to hear, but slow to answer.
5.14: If you have the knowledge, answer your neighbor; if not, put your hand over your mouth.
5.15: Honor and dishonor through talking! A man's tongue can be his downfall.
5.16: Be not called a detractor; use not your tongue for calumny;
5.17: For shame has been created for the thief, and the reproach of his neighbor for the double-tongued.

6.1: Say nothing harmful, small or great; be not a foe instead of a friend; A bad name and disgrace will you acquire: "That for the evil man with double tongue!"
6.2: Fall not into the grip of desire, lest, like fire, it consume your strength;
6.3: Your leaves it will eat, your fruits destroy, and you will be left a dry tree,
6.4: For contumacious desire destroys its owner and makes him the sport of his enemies.
6.5: A kind mouth multiplies friends, and gracious lips prompt friendly greetings.
6.6: Let your acquaintances be many, but one in a thousand your confidant.
6.7: When you gain a friend, first test him, and be not too ready to trust him.
6.8: For one sort of friend is a friend when it suits him, but he will not be with you in time of distress.
6.9: Another is a friend who becomes an enemy, and tells of the quarrel to your shame.
6.10: Another is a friend, a boon companion, who will not be with you when sorrow comes.
6.11: When things go well, he is your other self, and lords it over your servants;
6.12: But if you are brought low, he turns against you and avoids meeting you.
6.13: Keep away from your enemies; be on your guard with your friends.
6.14: A faithful friend is a sturdy shelter; he who finds one finds a treasure.
6.15: A faithful friend is beyond price, no sum can balance his worth.
6.16: A faithful friend is a life-saving remedy, such as he who fears God finds;
6.17: For he who fears God behaves accordingly, and his friend will be like himself.
6.18: My son, from your youth embrace discipline; thus will you find wisdom with graying hair.
6.19: As though plowing and sowing, draw close to her; then await her bountiful crops.
6.20: For in cultivating her you will labor but little, and soon you will eat of her fruits.
6.21: How irksome she is to the unruly! The fool cannot abide her.
6.22: She will be like a burdensome stone to test him, and he will not delay in casting her aside.
6.23: For discipline is like her name, she is not accessible to many.
6.24: Listen, my son, and heed my advice; refuse not my counsel.
6.25: Put your feet into her fetters, and your neck under her yoke.
6.26: Stoop your shoulders and carry her and be not irked at her bonds.
6.27: With all your soul draw close to her; with all your strength keep her ways.
6.28: Search her out, discover her; seek her and you will find her. Then when you have her, do not let her go;
6.29: Thus will you afterward find rest in her, and she will become your joy.
6.30: Her fetters will be your throne of majesty; her bonds, your purple cord.
6.31: You will wear her as your robe of glory, bear her as your splendid crown.
6.32: My son, if you wish, you can be taught; if you apply yourself, you will be shrewd.
6.33: If you are willing to listen, you will learn; if you give heed, you will be wise.
6.34: Frequent the company of the elders; whoever is wise, stay close to him.
6.35: Be eager to hear every godly discourse; let no wise saying escape you.
6.36: If you see a man of prudence, seek him out; let your feet wear away his doorstep!
6.37: Reflect on the precepts of the LORD, let his commandments be your constant meditation; Then he will enlighten your mind, and the wisdom you desire he will grant.

7.1: Do no evil, and evil will not overtake you;
7.2: avoid wickedness, and it will turn aside from you.
7.3: Sow not in the furrows of injustice, lest you harvest it sevenfold.
7.4: Seek not from the LORD authority, nor from the king a place of honor.
7.5: Parade not your justice before the Lord, and before the king flaunt not your wisdom.
7.6: Seek not to become a judge if you have not strength to root out crime, Or you will show favor to the ruler and mar your integrity.
7.7: Be guilty of no evil before the city's populace, nor disgrace yourself before the assembly.
7.8: Do not plot to repeat a sin; not even for one will you go unpunished.
7.9: Say not: "He will appreciate my many gifts; the Most High will accept my offerings."
7.10: Be not impatient in prayers, and neglect not the giving of alms.
7.11: Laugh not at an embittered man; be mindful of him who exalts and humbles.
7.12: Plot no mischief against your brother, nor against your friend and companion.
7.13: Delight not in telling lie after lie, for it never results in good.
7.14: Thrust not yourself into the deliberations of princes, and repeat not the words of your prayer.
7.15: Hate not laborious tasks, nor farming, which was ordained by the Most High.
7.16: Do not esteem yourself better than your fellows; remember, his wrath will not delay.
7.17: More and more, humble your pride; what awaits man is worms.
7.18: Barter not a friend for money, nor a dear brother for the gold of Ophir.
7.19: Dismiss not a sensible wife; a gracious wife is more precious than corals.
7.20: Mistreat not a servant who faithfully serves, nor a laborer who devotes himself to his task.
7.21: Let a wise servant be dear to you as your own self; refuse him not his freedom.
7.22: If you have livestock, look after them; if they are dependable, keep them.
7.23: If you have sons, chastise them; bend their necks from childhood.
7.24: If you have daughters, keep them chaste, and be not indulgent to them.
7.25: Giving your daughter in marriage ends a great task; but give her to a worthy man.
7.26: If you have a wife, let her not seem odious to you; but where there is ill-feeling, trust her not.
7.27: With your whole heart honor your father; your mother's birthpangs forget not.
7.28: Remember, of these parents you were born; what can you give them for all they gave you?
7.29: With all your soul, fear God, revere his priests.
7.30: With all your strength, love your Creator, forsake not his ministers.
7.31: Honor God and respect the priest; give him his portion as you have been commanded: First fruits and contributions, due sacrifices and holy offerings.
7.32: To the poor man also extend your hand, that your blessing may be complete;
7.33: Be generous to all the living, and withhold not your kindness from the dead.
7.34: Avoid not those who weep, but mourn with those who mourn;
7.35: Neglect not to visit the sick - for these things you will be loved.
7.36: In whatever you do, remember your last days, and you will never sin.

8.1: Contend not with an influential man, lest you fall into his power.
8.2: Quarrel not with a rich man, lest he pay out the price of your downfall; for gold has dazzled many, and perverts the character of princes.
8.3: Dispute not with a man of railing speech, heap no wood upon his fire.
8.4: Be not too familiar with an unruly man, lest he speak ill of your forebears.
8.5: Shame not a repentant sinner; remember, we all are guilty.
8.6: Insult no man when he is old, for some of us, too, will grow old.
8.7: Rejoice not when a man dies; remember, we are all to die.
8.8: Spurn not the discourse of the wise, but acquaint yourself with their proverbs; from them you will acquire the training to serve in the presence of princes.
8.9: Reject not the tradition of old men which they have learned from their fathers; from it you will obtain the knowledge how to answer in time of need.
8.10: Kindle not the coals of a sinner, lest you be consumed in his flaming fire.
8.11: Let not the impious man intimidate you; it will set him in ambush against you.
8.12: Lend not to one more powerful than yourself; and whatever you lend, count it as lost.
8.13: Go not surety beyond your means; think any pledge a debt you must pay.
8.14: Contend not at law with a judge, for he will settle it according to his whim.
8.15: Travel not with a ruthless man, lest he weigh you down with calamity; for he will go his own way straight, and through his folly you will perish with him.
8.16: Provoke no quarrel with a quick- tempered man, nor ride with him through the desert, for bloodshed is nothing to him; when there is no one to help you, he will destroy you.
8.17: Take no counsel with a fool, for he can keep nothing to himself.
8.18: Before a stranger do nothing that should be kept secret, for you know not what it will engender.
8.19: Open your heart to no man, and banish not your happiness.

9.1: Be not jealous of the wife of your bosom, lest you teach her to do evil against you.
9.2: Give no woman power over you to trample upon your dignity.
9.3: Be not intimate with a strange woman, lest you fall into her snares.
9.4: With a singing girl be not familiar, lest you be caught in her wiles.
9.5: Entertain no thoughts against a virgin, lest you be enmeshed in damages for her.
9.6: Give not yourself to harlots, lest you surrender your inheritance.
9.7: Gaze not about the lanes of the city and wander not through its squares;
9.8: Avert your eyes from a comely woman; gaze not upon the beauty of another's wife—through woman's beauty many perish, for lust for it burns like fire.
9.9: With a married woman dine not, recline not at table to drink by her side, lest your heart be drawn to her and you go down in blood to the grave.
9.10: Discard not an old friend, for the new one cannot equal him. A new friend is like new wine which you drink with pleasure only when it has aged.
9.11: Envy not a sinner's fame, for you know not what disaster awaits him.
9.12: Rejoice not at a proud man's success; remember he will not reach death unpunished.
9.13: Keep far from the man who has power to kill, and you will not be filled with the dread of death. But if you approach him, offend him not, lest he take away your life; know that you are stepping among snares and walking over a net.
9.14: As best you can, take your neighbors' measure, and associate with the wise.
9.15: With the learned be intimate; let all your conversation be about the law of the LORD.
9.16: Have just men for your table companions; in the fear of God be your glory.
9.17: Skilled artisans are esteemed for their deftness; but the ruler of his people is the skilled sage.
9.18: Feared in the city is the man of railing speech, and he who talks rashly is hated.

10.1: A wise magistrate lends stability to his people, and the government of a prudent man is well ordered.
10.2: As the people's judge, so are his ministers; as the head of a city, its inhabitants.
10.3: A wanton king destroys his people, but a city grows through the wisdom of its princes.
10.4: Sovereignty over the earth is in the hand of God, who raises up on it the man of the hour;
10.5: Sovereignty over every man is in the hand of God, who imparts his majesty to the ruler.
10.6: No matter the wrong, do no violence to your neighbor, and do not walk the path of arrogance.
10.7: Odious to the LORD and to men is arrogance, and the sin of oppression they both hate.
10.8: Dominion is transferred from one people to another because of the violence of the arrogant.
10.9: Why are dust and ashes proud? Even during life man's body decays;
10.10: A slight illness—the doctor jests, a king today—tomorrow he is dead.
10.11: When a man dies, he inherits corruption; worms and gnats and maggots.
10.12: The beginning of pride is man's stubbornness in withdrawing his heart from his Maker;
10.13: For pride is the reservoir of sin, a source which runs over with vice; because of it God sends unheard-of afflictions and brings men to utter ruin.
10.14: The thrones of the arrogant God overturns and establishes the lowly in their stead.
10.15: The roots of the proud God plucks up, to plant the humble in their place:
10.16: He breaks down their stem to the level of the ground, then digs their roots from the earth.
10.17: The traces of the proud God sweeps away and effaces the memory of them from the earth.
10.18: Insolence is not allotted to a man, nor stubborn anger to one born of woman.
10.19: Whose offspring can be in honor? Those of men. Which offspring are in honor? Those who fear God. Whose offspring can be in disgrace? Those of men. Which offspring are in disgrace? Those who transgress the commandments.
10.20: Among brethren their leader is in honor; he who fears God is in honor among his people.
10.21: Be it tenant or wayfarer, alien or pauper, his glory is the fear of the LORD.
10.22: It is not just to despise a man who is wise but poor, nor proper to honor any sinner.
10.23: The prince, the ruler, the judge are in honor; but none is greater than he who fears God.
10.24: When free men serve a prudent slave, the wise man does not complain.
10.25: Flaunt not your wisdom in managing your affairs, and boast not in your time of need.
10.26: Better the worker who has plenty of everything than the boaster who is without bread.
10.27: My son, with humility have self-esteem; prize yourself as you deserve.
10.28: Who will acquit him who condemns himself? Who will honor him who discredits himself?
10.29: The poor man is honored for his wisdom as the rich man is honored for his wealth;
10.30: Honored in poverty, how much more so in wealth! Dishonored in wealth, in poverty how much the more!

11.1: The poor man's wisdom lifts his head high and sets him among princes.
11.2: Praise not a man for his looks; despise not a man for his appearance.
11.3: Least is the bee among winged things, but she reaps the choicest of all harvests.
11.4: Mock not the worn cloak and jibe at no man's bitter day: For strange are the works of the LORD, hidden from men his deeds.
11.5: The oppressed often rise to a throne, and some that none would consider wear a crown.
11.6: The exalted often fall into utter disgrace; the honored are given into enemy hands.
11.7: Before investigating, find no fault; examine first, then criticize.
11.8: Before hearing, answer not, and interrupt no one in the middle of his speech.
11.9: Dispute not about what is not your concern; in the strife of the arrogant take no part.
11.10: My son, why increase your cares, since he who is avid for wealth will not be blameless? Even if you run after it, you will never overtake it; however you seek it, you will not find it.
11.11: One may toil and struggle and drive, and fall short all the more.
11.12: Another goes his way a weakling and a failure, with little strength and great misery—yet the eyes of the LORD look favorably upon him; he raises him free of the vile dust,
11.13: lifts up his head and exalts him to the amazement of the many.
11.14: Good and evil, life and death, poverty and riches, are from the LORD.
11.15: Wisdom and understanding and knowledge of affairs, love and virtuous paths are from the LORD.
11.16: Error and darkness were formed with sinners from their birth, and evil grows old with evildoers.
11.17: The LORD'S gift remains with the just; his favor brings continued success.
11.18: A man may become rich through a miser's life, and this is his allotted reward:
11.19: When he says: "I have found rest, now I will feast on my possessions," he does not know how long it will be till he dies and leaves them to others.
11.20: My son, hold fast to your duty, busy yourself with it, grow old while doing your task.
11.21: Admire not how sinners live, but trust in the LORD and wait for his light; for it is easy with the LORD suddenly, in an instant, to make a poor man rich.
11.22: God's blessing is the lot of the just man, and in due time his hopes bear fruit.
11.23: Say not: "What do I need? What further pleasure can be mine?"
11.24: Say not: "I am independent. What harm can come to me now?"
11.25: The day of prosperity makes one forget adversity; the day of adversity makes one forget prosperity.
11.26: For it is easy with the LORD on the day of death to repay man according to his deeds.
11.27: A moment's affliction brings forgetfulness of past delights; when a man dies, his life is revealed.
11.28: Call no man happy before his death, for by how he ends, a man is known.
11.29: Bring not every man into your house, for many are the snares of the crafty one;
11.30: though he seem like a bird confined in a cage, yet like a spy he will pick out the weak spots.
11.31: The talebearer turns good into evil; with a spark he sets many coals afire.
11.32: The evil man lies in wait for blood, and plots against your choicest possessions.
11.33: Avoid a wicked man, for he breeds only evil, lest you incur a lasting stain.
11.34: Lodge a stranger with you, and he will subvert your course, and make a stranger of you to your own household.

12.1: If you do good, know for whom you are doing it, and your kindness will have its effect.
12.2: Do good to the just man and reward will be yours, if not from him, from the LORD.
12.3: No good comes to him who gives comfort to the wicked, nor is it an act of mercy that he does.
12.4: Give to the good man, refuse the sinner; refresh the downtrodden, give nothing to the proud man.
12.5: No arms for combat should you give him, lest he use them against yourself;
12.6: with twofold evil you will meet for every good deed you do for him.
12.7: The Most High himself hates sinners, and upon the wicked he takes vengeance.
12.8: In our prosperity we cannot know our friends; in adversity an enemy will not remain concealed.
12.9: When a man is successful even his enemy is friendly; in adversity even his friend disappears.
12.10: Never trust your enemy, for his wickedness is like corrosion in bronze.
12.11: Even though he acts humbly and peaceably toward you, take care to be on your guard against him. Rub him as one polishes a brazen mirror, and you will find that there is still corrosion.
12.12: Let him not stand near you, lest he oust you and take your place. Let him not sit at your right hand, lest he then demand your seat, and in the end you appreciate my advice, when you groan with regret, as I warned you.
12.13: Who pities a snake charmer when he is bitten, or anyone who goes near a wild beast?
12.14: So is it with the companion of the proud man, who is involved in his sins:
12.15: While you stand firm, he makes no bold move; but if you slip, he cannot hold back.
12.16: With his lips an enemy speaks sweetly, but in his heart he schemes to plunge you into the abyss. Though your enemy has tears in his eyes, if given the chance, he will never have enough of your blood.
12.17: If evil comes upon you, you will find him at hand; feigning to help, he will trip you up,
12.18: then he will nod his head and clap his hands and hiss repeatedly, and show his true face.

13.1: He who touches pitch blackens his hand; he who associates with an impious man learns his ways.
13.2: Bear no burden too heavy for you; go with no one greater or wealthier than yourself. How can the earthen pot go with the metal cauldron? When they knock together, the pot will be smashed:
13.3: The rich man does wrong and boasts of it, the poor man is wronged and begs forgiveness.
13.4: As long as the rich man can use you he will enslave you, but when you are exhausted, he will abandon you.
13.5: As long as you have anything he will speak fair words to you, and with smiles he will win your confidence;
13.6: When he needs something from you he will cajole you, then without regret he will impoverish you.
13.7: While it serves his purpose he will beguile you, then twice or three times he will terrify you; when later he sees you he will pass you by, and shake his head over you.
13.8: Guard against being presumptuous; be not as those who lack sense.
13.9: When invited by a man of influence, keep your distance; then he will urge you all the more.
13.10: Be not bold with him lest you be rebuffed, but keep not too far away lest you be forgotten.
13.11: Engage not freely in discussion with him, trust not his many words; for by prolonged talk he will test you, and though smiling he will probe you.
13.12: Mercilessly he will make of you a laughingstock, and will not refrain from injury or chains.
13.13: Be on your guard and take care never to accompany men of violence.
13.14: Every living thing loves its own kind, every man a man like himself.
13.15: Every being is drawn to its own kind; with his own kind every man associates.
13.16: Is a wolf ever allied with a lamb? So it is with the sinner and the just.
13.17: Can there be peace between the hyena and the dog? Or between the rich and the poor can there be peace?
13.18: Lion's prey are the wild asses of the desert; so too the poor are feeding grounds for the rich.
13.19: A proud man abhors lowliness; so does the rich man abhor the poor.
13.20: When a rich man stumbles he is supported by a friend; when a poor man trips he is pushed down by a friend.
13.21: Many are the supporters for a rich man when he speaks; though what he says is odious, it wins approval. When a poor man speaks they make sport of him; he speaks wisely and no attention is paid him.
13.22: A rich man speaks and all are silent, his wisdom they extol to the clouds. A poor man speaks and they say: "Who is that?" If he slips they cast him down.
13.23: Wealth is good when there is no sin; but poverty is evil by the standards of the proud.
13.24: The heart of a man changes his countenance, either for good or for evil.
13.25: The sign of a good heart is a cheerful countenance; withdrawn and perplexed is the laborious schemer.

14.1: Happy the man whose mouth brings him no grief, who is not stung by remorse for sin.
14.2: Happy the man whose conscience does not reproach him, who has not lost hope.
14.3: Wealth ill becomes the mean man; and to the miser, of what use is gold?
14.4: What he denies himself he collects for others, and in his possessions a stranger will revel.
14.5: To whom will he be generous who is stingy with himself and does not enjoy what is his own?
14.6: None is more stingy than he who is stingy with himself; he punishes his own miserliness.
14.7: If ever he is generous, it is by mistake; and in the end he displays his greed.
14.8: In the miser's opinion his share is too small;
14.9: he refuses his neighbor and brings ruin on himself.
14.10: The miser's eye is rapacious for bread, but on his own table he sets it stale.
14.11: My son, use freely whatever you have and enjoy it as best you can;
14.12: Remember that death does not tarry, nor have you been told the grave's appointed time.
14.13: Before you die, be good to your friend, and give him a share in what you possess.
14.14: Deprive not yourself of present good things, let no choice portion escape you.
14.15: Will you not leave your riches to others, and your earnings to be divided by lot?
14.16: Give, take, and treat yourself well, for in the nether world there are no joys to seek.
14.17: All flesh grows old, like a garment; the age-old law is: All must die.
14.18: As with the leaves that grow on a vigorous tree: one falls off and another sprouts - So with the generations of flesh and blood: one dies and another is born.
14.19: All man's works will perish in decay, and his handiwork will follow after him.
14.20: Happy the man who meditates on wisdom, and reflects on knowledge;
14.21: Who ponders her ways in his heart, and understands her paths;
14.22: Who pursues her like a scout, and lies in wait at her entry way;
14.23: Who peeps through her windows, and listens at her doors;
14.24: Who encamps near her house, and fastens his tent pegs next to her walls;
14.25: Who pitches his tent beside her, and lives as her welcome neighbor;
14.26: Who builds his nest in her leafage, and lodges in her branches;
14.27: Who takes shelter with her from the heat, and dwells in her home.

15.1: He who fears the LORD will do this; he who is practiced in the law will come to wisdom.
15.2: Motherlike she will meet him, like a young bride she will embrace him,
15.3: Nourish him with the bread of understanding, and give him the water of learning to drink.
15.4: He will lean upon her and not fall, he will trust in her and not be put to shame.
15.5: She will exalt him above his fellows; in the assembly she will make him eloquent.
15.6: Joy and gladness he will find, an everlasting name inherit.
15.7: Worthless men will not attain to her, haughty men will not behold her.
15.8: Far from the impious is she, not to be spoken of by liars.
15.9: Unseemly is praise on a sinner's lips, for it is not accorded to him by God.
15.10: But praise is offered by the wise man's tongue; its rightful steward will proclaim it.
15.11: Say not: "It was God's doing that I fell away"; for what he hates he does not do.
15.12: Say not: "It was he who set me astray"; for he has no need of wicked man.
15.13: Abominable wickedness the LORD hates, he does not let it befall those who fear him.
15.14: When God, in the beginning, created man, he made him subject to his own free choice.
15.15: If you choose you can keep the commandments; it is loyalty to do his will.
15.16: There are set before you fire and water; to whichever you choose, stretch forth your hand.
15.17: Before man are life and death, whichever he chooses shall be given him.
15.18: Immense is the wisdom of the LORD; he is mighty in power, and all-seeing.
15.19: The eyes of God see all he has made; he understands man's every deed.
15.20: No man does he command to sin, to none does he give strength for lies.

16.1: Desire not a brood of worthless children, nor rejoice in wicked offspring.
16.2: Many though they be, exult not in them if they have not the fear of the LORD.
16.3: Count not on their length of life, have no hope in their future. For one can be better than a thousand; rather die childless than have godless children!
16.4: Through one wise man can a city be peopled; through a clan of rebels it becomes desolate.
16.5: Many such things has my eye seen, even more than these has my ear heard.
16.6: Against a sinful band fire is enkindled, upon a godless people wrath flames out.
16.7: He forgave not the leaders of old who rebelled long ago in their might;
16.8: He spared not the neighbors of Lot whom he detested for their pride;
16.9: Nor did he spare the doomed people who were uprooted because of their sin;
16.10: Nor the six hundred thousand foot soldiers who perished for the impiety of their hearts.
16.11: And had there been but one stiffnecked man, it were a wonder had he gone unpunished. For mercy and anger alike are with him who remits and forgives, though on the wicked alights his wrath.
16.12: Great as his mercy is his punishment; he judges men, each according to his deeds.
16.13: A criminal does not escape with his plunder; a just man's hope God does not leave unfulfilled.
16.14: Whoever does good has his reward, which each receives according to his deeds.
16.15: Say not: "I am hidden from God; in heaven who remembers me? Among so many people I cannot be known; what am I in the world of spirits?
16.16: Behold, the heavens, the heaven of heavens, the earth and the abyss tremble at his visitation;
16.17: The roots of the mountains, the earth's foundations, at his mere glance, quiver and quake.
16.18: Of me, therefore, he will take no thought; with my ways who will concern himself?
16.19: If I sin, no eye will see me; if all in secret I am disloyal, who is to know?
16.20: Who tells him of just deeds and what could I expect for doing my duty?"
16.21: Such are the thoughts of senseless men, which only the foolish knave will think.
16.22: Hearken to me, my son, take my advice, apply your mind to my words,
16.23: While I propose measured wisdom, and impart accurate knowledge.
16.24: When at the first God created his works and, as he made them, assigned their tasks,
16.25: He ordered for all time what they were to do and their domains from generation to generation. They were not to hunger, nor grow weary, nor ever cease from their tasks.
16.26: Not one should ever crowd its neighbor, nor should they ever disobey his word.
16.27: Then the LORD looked upon the earth, and filled it with his blessings.
16.28: Its surface he covered with all manner of life which must return into it again.

17.1: The LORD from the earth created man, and in his own image he made him.
17.2: Limited days of life he gives him and makes him return to earth again.
17.3: He endows man with a strength of his own, and with power over all things else on earth.
17.4: He puts the fear of him in all flesh, and gives him rule over beasts and birds.
17.5: He forms men's tongues and eyes and ears, and imparts to them an understanding heart.
17.6: With wisdom and knowledge he fills them; good and evil he shows them.
17.7: He looks with favor upon their hearts, and shows them his glorious works,
17.8: That they may describe the wonders of his deeds and praise his holy name.
17.9: He has set before them knowledge, a law of life as their inheritance;
17.10: An everlasting covenant he has made with them, his commandments he has revealed to them.
17.11: His majestic glory their eyes beheld, his glorious voice their ears heard.
17.12: He says to them, "Avoid all evil"; each of them he gives precepts about his fellow men.
17.13: Their ways are ever known to him, they cannot be hidden from his eyes.
17.14: Over every nation he places a ruler, but the LORD'S own portion is Israel.
17.15: All their actions are clear as the sun to him, his eyes are ever upon their ways.
17.16: Their wickedness cannot be hidden from him; all of their sins are before the LORD.
17.17: A man's goodness God cherishes like a signet ring, a man's virtue, like the apple of his eye.
17.18: Later he will rise up and repay them, and requite each one of them as they deserve.
17.19: But to the penitent he provides a way back, he encourages those who are losing hope!
17.20: Return to the LORD and give up sin, pray to him and make your offenses few.
17.21: Turn again to the Most High and away from sin, hate intensely what he loathes;
17.22: Who in the nether world can glorify the Most High in place of the living who offer their praise?
17.23: No more can the dead give praise than those who have never lived; they glorify the LORD who are alive and well.
17.24: How great the mercy of the LORD, his forgiveness of those who return to him!
17.25: The like cannot be found in men, for not immortal is any son of man.
17.26: Is anything brighter than the sun? Yet it can be eclipsed. How obscure then the thoughts of flesh and blood!
17.27: God watches over the hosts of highest heaven, while all men are dust and ashes.

18.1: The Eternal is the judge of all things without exception; the LORD alone is just.
18.2: Whom has he made equal to describing his works, and who can probe his mighty deeds?
18.3: Who can measure his majestic power, or exhaust the tale of his mercies?
18.4: One cannot lessen, nor increase, nor penetrate the wonders of the LORD.
18.5: When a man ends he is only beginning, and when he stops he is still bewildered.
18.6: What is man, of what worth is he? the good, the evil in him, what are these?
18.7: The sum of a man's days is great if it reaches a hundred years:
18.8: Like a drop of sea water, like a grain of sand, so are these few years among the days of eternity.
18.9: That is why the LORD is patient with men and showers upon them his mercy.
18.10: He sees and understands that their death is grievous, and so he forgives them all the more.
18.11: Man may be merciful to his fellow man, but the LORD'S mercy reaches all flesh,
18.12: Reproving, admonishing, teaching, as a shepherd guides his flock;
18.13: Merciful to those who accept his guidance, who are diligent in his precepts.
18.14: My son, to your charity add no reproach, nor spoil any gift by harsh words.
18.15: Like dew that abates a burning wind, so does a word improve a gift.
18.16: Sometimes the word means more than the gift; both are offered by a kindly man.
18.17: Only a fool upbraids before giving; a grudging gift wears out the expectant eyes.
18.18: Be informed before speaking; before sickness prepare the cure.
18.19: Before you are judged, seek merit for yourself, and at the time of visitation you will have a ransom.
18.20: Before you have fallen, humble yourself; when you have sinned, show repentance.
18.21: Delay not to forsake sins, neglect it not till you are in distress.
18.22: Let nothing prevent the prompt payment of your vows; wait not to fulfill them when you are dying.
18.23: Before making a vow have the means to fulfill it; be not one who tries the LORD.
18.24: Think of wrath and the day of death, the time of vengeance when he will hide his face.
18.25: Remember the time of hunger in the time of plenty, poverty and want in the day of wealth.
18.26: Between morning and evening the weather changes; before the LORD all things are fleeting.
18.27: A wise man is circumspect in all things; when sin is rife he keeps himself from wrongdoing.
18.28: Any learned man should make wisdom known, and he who attains to her should declare her praise;
18.29: Those trained in her words must show their wisdom, dispensing sound proverbs like life-giving waters.
18.30: Go not after your lusts, but keep your desires in check.
18.31: If you satisfy your lustful appetites they will make you the sport of your enemies.
18.32: Have no joy in the pleasures of a moment which bring on poverty redoubled;
18.33: Become not a glutton and a winebibber with nothing in your purse.

19.1: He who does so grows no richer; he who wastes the little he has will be stripped bare.
19.2: Wine and women make the mind giddy, and the companion of harlots becomes reckless.
19.3: He who lightly trusts in them has no sense, and he who strays after them sins against his own life.
19.4: Rottenness and worms will possess him, for contumacious desire destroys its owner.
19.5: He who gloats over evil will meet with evil, and he who repeats an evil report has no sense.
19.6: Never repeat gossip, and you will not be reviled.
19.7: Tell nothing to friend or foe; if you have a fault, reveal it not,
19.8: For he who hears it will hold it against you, and in time become your enemy.
19.9: Let anything you hear die within you; be assured it will not make you burst.
19.10: When a fool hears something, he is in labor, like a woman giving birth to a child.
19.11: Like an arrow lodged in a man's thigh is gossip in the breast of a fool.
19.12: Admonish your friend - he may not have done it; and if he did, that he may not do it again.
19.13: Admonish your neighbor - he may not have said it; and if he did, that he may not say it again.
19.14: Admonish your friend - often it may be slander; every story you must not believe.
19.15: Then, too, a man can slip and not mean it; who has not sinned with his tongue?
19.16: Admonish your neighbor before you break with him; thus will you fulfill the law of the Most High.
19.17: All wisdom is fear of the LORD; perfect wisdom is the fulfillment of the law.
19.18: The knowledge of wickedness is not wisdom, nor is there prudence in the counsel of sinners.
19.19: There is a shrewdness that is detestable, while the simple man may be free from sin.
19.20: There are those with little understanding who fear God, and those of great intelligence who violate the law.
19.21: There is a shrewdness keen but dishonest, which by duplicity wins a judgment.
19.22: There is the wicked man who is bowed in grief, but is full of guile within;
19.23: He bows his head and feigns not to hear, but when not observed, he will take advantage of you:
19.24: Even though his lack of strength keeps him from sinning, when he finds the opportunity, he will do harm.
19.25: One can tell a man by his appearance; a wise man is known as such when first met.
19.26: A man's attire, his hearty laughter and his gait, proclaim him for what he is.

20.1: An admonition can be inopportune, and a man may be wise to hold his peace.
20.2: It is much better to admonish than to lose one's temper, for one who admits his fault will be kept from disgrace.
20.3: Like a eunuch lusting for intimacy with a maiden is he who does right under compulsion.
20.4: One man is silent and is thought wise, another is talkative and is disliked.
20.5: One man is silent because he has nothing to say; another is silent, biding his time.
20.6: A wise man is silent till the right time comes, but a boasting fool ignores the proper time.
20.7: He who talks too much is detested; he who pretends to authority is hated.
20.8: Some misfortunes bring success; some things gained are a man's loss.
20.9: Some gifts do one no good, and some must be paid back double.
20.10: Humiliation can follow fame, while from obscurity a man can lift up his head.
20.11: A man may buy much for little, but pay for it seven times over.
20.12: A wise man makes himself popular by a few words, but fools pour forth their blandishments in vain.
20.13: A gift from a rogue will do you no good, for in his eyes his one gift is equal to seven.
20.14: He gives little and criticizes often, and like a crier he shouts aloud. He lends today, he asks it back tomorrow; hateful indeed is such a man.
20.15: A fool has no friends, nor thanks for his generosity.
20.16: Those who eat his bread have an evil tongue. How many times they laugh him to scorn!
20.17: A fall to the ground is less sudden than a slip of the tongue; that is why the downfall of the wicked comes so quickly.
20.18: Insipid food is the untimely tale; the unruly are always ready to offer it.
20.19: A proverb when spoken by a fool is unwelcome, for he does not utter it at the proper time.
20.20: A man through want may be unable to sin, yet in this tranquility he cannot rest.
20.21: One may lose his life through shame, and perish through a fool's intimidation.
20.22: A man makes a promise to a friend out of shame, and has him for his enemy needlessly.
20.23: A lie is a foul blot in a man, yet it is constantly on the lips of the unruly.
20.24: Better a thief than an inveterate liar, yet both will suffer disgrace.
20.25: A liar's way leads to dishonor, his shame remains ever with him.
20.26: A wise man advances himself by his words, a prudent man pleases the great.
20.27: He who works his land has abundant crops, he who pleases the great is pardoned his faults.
20.28: Favors and gifts blind the eyes; like a muzzle over the mouth they silence reproof.
20.29: Hidden wisdom and unseen treasure—of what value is either?
20.30: Better the man who hides his folly than the one who hides his wisdom.

21.1: My son, if you have sinned, do so no more, and for your past sins pray to be forgiven.
21.2: Flee from sin as from a serpent, that will bite you if you go near it; its teeth are lion's teeth, destroying the souls of men.
21.3: Every offense is a two-edged sword; when it cuts, there can be no healing.
21.4: Violence and arrogance wipe out wealth; so too a proud man's home is destroyed.
21.5: Prayer from a poor man's lips is heard at once, and justice is quickly granted him.
21.6: He who hates correction walks the sinner's path, but he who fears the LORD repents in his heart.
21.7: Widely known is the boastful speaker, but the wise man knows his own faults.
21.8: He who builds his house with another's money is collecting stones for his funeral mound.
21.9: A band of criminals is like a bundle of tow; they will end in a flaming fire.
21.10: The path of sinners is smooth stones that end in the depths of the nether world.
21.11: He who keeps the law controls his impulses; he who is perfect in fear of the LORD has wisdom.
21.12: He can never be taught who is not shrewd, but one form of shrewdness is thoroughly bitter.
21.13: A wise man's knowledge wells up in a flood, and his counsel, like a living spring.
21.14: A fool's mind is like a broken jar—no knowledge at all can it hold.
21.15: When an intelligent man hears words of wisdom, he approves them and adds to them; the wanton hears them with scorn and casts them behind his back.
21.16: A fool's chatter is like a load on a journey, but there is charm to be found upon the lips of the wise.
21.17: The views of a prudent man are sought in an assembly, and his words are considered with care.
21.18: Like a house in ruins is wisdom to a fool; the stupid man knows it only as inscrutable words.
21.19: Like fetters on the legs is learning to a fool, like a manacle on his right hand.
21.20: A fool raises his voice in laughter, but the prudent man at the most smiles gently.
21.21: Like a chain of gold is learning to a wise man, like a bracelet on his right arm.
21.22: The fool steps boldly into a house, while the well-bred man remains outside.
21.23: A boor peeps through the doorway of a house, but a cultured man keeps his glance cast down.
21.24: It is rude for one to listen at a door; a cultured man would be overwhelmed by the disgrace of it.
21.25: The lips of the impious talk of what is not their concern, but the words of the prudent are carefully weighed.
21.26: Fools' thoughts are in their mouths, wise men's words are in their hearts.
21.27: When a godless man curses his adversary, he really curses himself.
21.28: A slanderer besmirches himself and is hated by his neighbors.

22.1: The sluggard is like a stone in the mud; everyone hisses at his disgrace.
22.2: The sluggard is like a lump of dung; whoever touches him wipes his hands.
22.3: An unruly child is a disgrace to its father; if it be a daughter she brings him to poverty.
22.4: A thoughtful daughter becomes a treasure to her husband, a shameless one is her father's grief.
22.5: A hussy shames her father and her husband; by both she is despised.
22.6: Like a song in time of mourning is inopportune talk, but lashes and discipline are at all times wisdom.
22.7: Teaching a fool is like gluing a broken pot, or like disturbing a man in the depths of sleep;
22.8: He talks with a slumberer who talks with a fool, for when it is over, he will say, "What was that?"
22.9: Weep over the dead man, for his light has gone out; weep over the fool, for sense has left him.
22.10: Weep but a little over the dead man, for he is at rest; but worse than death is the life of a fool.
22.11: Seven days of mourning for the dead, but for the wicked fool a whole lifetime.
22.12: Speak but seldom with the stupid man, be not the companion of a brute;
22.13: Beware of him lest you have trouble and be spattered when he shakes himself; Turn away from him and you will find rest and not be wearied by his lack of sense.
22.14: What is heavier than lead, and what is its name but "Fool"?
22.15: Sand and salt and an iron mass are easier to bear than a stupid man.
22.16: Masonry bonded with wooden beams is not loosened by an earthquake; Neither is a resolve constructed with careful deliberation shaken in a moment of fear.
22.17: A resolve that is backed by prudent understanding is like the polished surface of a smooth wall.
22.18: Small stones lying on an open height will not remain when the wind blows; Neither can a timid resolve based on foolish plans withstand fear of any kind.
22.19: One who jabs the eye brings tears: he who pierces the heart bares its feelings.
22.20: He who throws stones at birds drives them away, and he who insults a friend breaks up the friendship.
22.21: Should you draw a sword against a friend, despair not, it can be undone.
22.22: Should you speak sharply to a friend, fear not, you can be reconciled. But a contemptuous insult, a confidence broken, or a treacherous attack will drive away any friend.
22.23: Make fast friends with a man while he is poor; thus will you enjoy his prosperity with him. In time of trouble remain true to him, so as to share in his inheritance when it comes.
22.24: Before flames burst forth an oven smokes; so does abuse come before bloodshed.
22.25: From a friend in need of support no one need hide in shame;
22.26: But from him who brings harm to his friend all will stand aloof who hear of it.
22.27: Who will set a guard over my mouth, and upon my lips an effective seal, That I may not fail through them, that my tongue may not destroy me?

23.1: LORD, Father and Master of my life, permit me not to fall by them!
23.2: Who will apply the lash to my thoughts, to my mind the rod of discipline, That my failings may not be spared, nor the sins of my heart overlooked;
23.3: Lest my failings increase, and my sins be multiplied; Lest I succumb to my foes, and my enemy rejoice over me?
23.4: LORD, Father and God of my life, abandon me not into their control!
23.5: A brazen look allow me not; ward off passion from my heart,
23.6: Let not the lustful cravings of the flesh master me, surrender me not to shameless desires.
23.7: Give heed, my children, to the instruction that I pronounce, for he who keeps it will not be enslaved.
23.8: Through his lips is the sinner ensnared; the railer and the arrogant man fall thereby.
23.9: Let not your mouth form the habit of swearing, or becoming too familiar with the Holy Name.
23.10: Just as a slave that is constantly under scrutiny will not be without welts, So one who swears continually by the Holy Name will not remain free from sin.
23.11: A man who often swears heaps up obligations; the scourge will never be far from his house. If he swears in error, he incurs guilt; if he neglects his obligation, his sin is doubly great. If he swears without reason he cannot be found just, and all his house will suffer affliction.
23.12: There are words which merit death; may they never be heard among Jacob's heirs. For all such words are foreign to the devout, who do not wallow in sin.
23.13: Let not your mouth become used to coarse talk, for in it lies sinful matter.
23.14: Keep your father and mother in mind when you sit among the mighty, Lest in their presence you commit a blunder and disgrace your upbringing, By wishing you had never been born or cursing the day of your birth.
23.15: A man who has the habit of abusive language will never mature in character as long as he lives.
23.16: Two types of men multiply sins, a third draws down wrath; For burning passion is a blazing fire, not to be quenched till it burns itself out: A man given to sins of the flesh, who never stops until the fire breaks forth;
23.17: The rake to whom all bread is sweet and who is never through till he dies;
23.18: And the man who dishonors his marriage bed and says to himself "Who can see me? Darkness surrounds me, walls hide me; no one sees me; why should I fear to sin?" Of the Most High he is not mindful,
23.19: fearing only the eyes of men; He does not understand that the eyes of the LORD, ten thousand times brighter than the sun, Observe every step a man takes and peer into hidden corners.
23.20: He who knows all things before they exist still knows them all after they are made.
23.21: Such a man will be punished in the streets of the city; when he least expects it, he will be apprehended.
23.22: So also with the woman who is unfaithful to her husband and offers as heir her son by a stranger.
23.23: First, she has disobeyed the law of the Most High; secondly, she has wronged her husband; Thirdly, in her wanton adultery she has borne children by another man.
23.24: Such a woman will be dragged before the assembly, and her punishment will extend to her children;
23.25: Her children will not take root; her branches will not bring forth fruit.
23.26: She will leave an accursed memory; her disgrace will never be blotted out.
23.27: Thus all who dwell on the earth shall know, and all who inhabit the world shall understand, That nothing is better than the fear of the LORD, nothing more salutary than to obey his commandments.

24.1: Wisdom sings her own praises, before her own people she proclaims her glory;
24.2: In the assembly of the Most High she opens her mouth, in the presence of his hosts she declares her worth:
24.3: "From the mouth of the Most High I came forth, and mistlike covered the earth.
24.4: In the highest heavens did I dwell, my throne on a pillar of cloud.
24.5: The vault of heaven I compassed alone, through the deep abyss I wandered.
24.6: Over waves of the sea, over all the land, over every people and nation I held sway.
24.7: Among all these I sought a resting place; in whose inheritance should I abide?
24.8: "Then the Creator of all gave me his command, and he who formed me chose the spot for my tent, Saying, 'In Jacob make your dwelling, in Israel your inheritance.'
24.9: Before all ages, in the beginning, he created me, and through all ages I shall not cease to be.
24.10: In the holy tent I ministered before him, and in Zion I fixed my abode.
24.11: Thus in the chosen city he has given me rest, in Jerusalem is my domain.
24.12: I have struck root among the glorious people, in the portion of the LORD, his heritage.
24.13: "Like a cedar on Lebanon I am raised aloft, like a cypress on Mount Hermon,
24.14: Like a palm tree in En-gedi, like a rosebush in Jericho, Like a fair olive tree in the field, like a plane tree growing beside the water.
24.15: Like cinnamon, or fragrant balm, or precious myrrh, I give forth perfume; Like galbanum and onycha and sweet spices, like the odor of incense in the holy place.
24.16: I spread out my branches like a terebinth, my branches so bright and so graceful.
24.17: I bud forth delights like the vine, my blossoms become fruit fair and rich.
24.18: Come to me, all you that yearn for me, and be filled with my fruits;
24.19: You will remember me as sweeter than honey, better to have than the honeycomb.
24.20: He who eats of me will hunger still, he who drinks of me will thirst for more;
24.21: He who obeys me will not be put to shame, he who serves me will never fail."
24.22: All this is true of the book of the Most High's covenant, the law which Moses commanded us as an inheritance for the community of Jacob.
24.23: It overflows, like the Pishon, with wisdom - like the Tigris in the days of the new fruits.
24.24: It runs over, like the Euphrates, with understanding, like the Jordan at harvest time.
24.25: It sparkles like the Nile with knowledge, like the Gihon at vintage time.
24.26: The first man never finished comprehending wisdom, nor will the last succeed in fathoming her.
24.27: For deeper than the sea are her thoughts; her counsels, than the great abyss.
24.28: Now I, like a rivulet from her stream, channeling the waters into a garden,
24.29: Said to myself, "I will water my plants, my flower bed I will drench"; And suddenly this rivulet of mine became a river, then this stream of mine, a sea.
24.30: Thus do I send my teachings forth shining like the dawn, to become known afar off.
24.31: Thus do I pour out instruction like prophecy and bestow it on generations to come.

25.1: With three things I am delighted, for they are pleasing to the LORD and to men: Harmony among brethren, friendship among neighbors, and the mutual love of husband and wife.
25.2: Three kinds of men I hate; their manner of life I loathe indeed: A proud pauper, a rich dissembler, and an old man lecherous in his dotage.
25.3: What you have not saved in your youth, how will you acquire in your old age?
25.4: How becoming to the gray-haired is judgment, and a knowledge of counsel to those on in years!
25.5: How becoming to the aged is wisdom, understanding and prudence to the venerable!
25.6: The crown of old men is wide experience; their glory, the fear of the LORD.
25.7: There are nine who come to my mind as blessed, a tenth whom my tongue proclaims: The man who finds joy in his children, and he who lives to see his enemies' downfall.
25.8: Happy is he who dwells with a sensible wife, and he who plows not like a donkey yoked with an ox. Happy is he who sins not with his tongue, and he who serves not his inferior.
25.9: Happy is he who finds a friend and he who speaks to attentive ears.
25.10: He who finds wisdom is great indeed, but not greater than he who fears the LORD.
25.11: Fear of the LORD surpasses all else. Its possessor is beyond compare.
25.12: Worst of all wounds is that of the heart, worst of all evils is that of a woman.
25.13: Worst of all sufferings is that from one's foes, worst of all vengeance is that of one's enemies:
25.14: No poison worse than that of a serpent, no venom greater than that of a woman.
25.15: With a dragon or a lion I would rather dwell than live with an evil woman.
25.16: Wickedness changes a woman's looks, and makes her sullen as a female bear.
25.17: When her husband sits among his neighbors, a bitter sigh escapes him unawares.
25.18: There is scarce any evil like that in a woman; may she fall to the lot of the sinner!
25.19: Like a sandy hill to aged feet is a railing wife to a quiet man.
25.20: Stumble not through woman's beauty, nor be greedy for her wealth;
25.21: The man is a slave, in disgrace and shame, when a wife supports her husband.
25.22: Depressed mind, saddened face, broken heart - this from an evil wife. Feeble hands and quaking knees - from a wife who brings no happiness to her husband.
25.23: In woman was sin's beginning, and because of her we all die.
25.24: Allow water no outlet, and be not indulgent to an erring wife.
25.25: If she walks not by your side, cut her away from you.

26.1: Happy the husband of a good wife, twice-lengthened are his days;
26.2: A worthy wife brings joy to her husband, peaceful and full is his life.
26.3: A good wife is a generous gift bestowed upon him who fears the LORD;
26.4: Be he rich or poor, his heart is content, and a smile is ever on his face.
26.5: There are three things at which my heart quakes, a fourth before which I quail: Though false charges in public, trial before all the people, and lying testimony are harder to bear than death,
26.6: A jealous wife is heartache and mourning and a scourging tongue like the other three.
26.7: A bad wife is a chafing yoke; he who marries her seizes a scorpion.
26.8: A drunken wife arouses great anger, for she does not hide her shame.
26.9: By her eyelids and her haughty stare an unchaste wife can be recognized.
26.10: Keep a strict watch over an unruly wife, lest, finding an opportunity, she make use of it;
26.11: Follow close if her eyes are bold, and be not surprised if she betrays you:
26.12: As a thirsty traveler with eager mouth drinks from any water that he finds, So she settles down before every tent peg and opens her quiver for every arrow.
26.13: A gracious wife delights her husband, her thoughtfulness puts flesh on his bones;
26.14: A gift from the LORD is her governed speech, and her firm virtue is of surpassing worth.
26.15: Choicest of blessings is a modest wife, priceless her chaste person.
26.16: Like the sun rising in the LORD'S heavens, the beauty of a virtuous wife is the radiance of her home.
26.17: Like the light which shines above the holy lampstand, are her beauty of face and graceful figure.
26.18: Golden columns on silver bases are her shapely limbs and steady feet.
26.19: These two bring grief to my heart, and the third arouses my horror: A wealthy man reduced to want; illustrious men held in contempt; And the man who passes from justice to sin, for whom the LORD makes ready the sword.
26.20: A merchant can hardly remain upright, nor a shopkeeper free from sin.

27.1: For the sake of profit many sin, and the struggle for wealth blinds the eyes.
27.2: Like a peg driven between fitted stones, between buying and selling sin is wedged in.
27.3: Unless you earnestly hold fast to the fear of the LORD, suddenly your house will be thrown down.
27.4: When a sieve is shaken, the husks appear; so do a man's faults when he speaks.
27.5: As the test of what the potter molds is in the furnace, so in his conversation is the test of a man.
27.6: The fruit of a tree shows the care it has had; so too does a man's speech disclose the bent of his mind.
27.7: Praise no man before he speaks, for it is then that men are tested.
27.8: If you strive after justice you will attain it, and put it on like a splendid robe.
27.9: Birds nest with their own kind, and fidelity comes to those who live by it.
27.10: As a lion crouches in wait for prey, so do sins for evildoers.
27.11: Ever wise are the discourses of the devout, but the godless man, like the moon, is inconstant.
27.12: Limit the time you spend among fools, but frequent the company of thoughtful men.
27.13: The conversation of the wicked is offensive, their laughter is wanton guilt.
27.14: Their oath-filled talk makes the hair stand on end, their brawls make one stop one's ears.
27.15: Wrangling among the haughty ends in bloodshed, their cursing is painful to hear.
27.16: He who betrays a secret cannot be trusted, he will never find an intimate friend.
27.17: Cherish your friend, keep faith with him; but if you betray his confidence, follow him not;
27.18: For as an enemy might kill a man, you have killed your neighbor's friendship.
27.19: Like a bird released from the hand, you have let your friend go and cannot recapture him;
27.20: Follow him not, for he is far away, he has fled like a gazelle from the trap.
27.21: A wound can be bound up, and an insult forgiven, but he who betrays secrets does hopeless damage.
27.22: He who has shifty eyes plots mischief and no one can ward him off;
27.23: In your presence he uses honeyed talk, and admires your every word, But later he changes his tone and twists your words to your ruin.
27.24: There is nothing that I hate so much, and the LORD hates him as well.
27.25: As a stone falls back on him who throws it up, so a blow struck in treachery injures more than one.
27.26: As he who digs a pit falls into it, and he who lays a snare is caught in it,
27.27: Whoever does harm will be involved in it without knowing how it came upon him.
27.28: Mockery and abuse will be the lot of the proud, and vengeance lies in wait for them like a lion.
27.29: The trap seizes those who rejoice in pitfalls, and pain will consume them before they die;
27.30: Wrath and anger are hateful things, yet the sinner hugs them tight.

28.1: The vengeful will suffer the LORD'S vengeance, for he remembers their sins in detail.
28.2: Forgive your neighbor's injustice; then when you pray, your own sins will be forgiven.
28.3: Should a man nourish anger against his fellows and expect healing from the LORD?
28.4: Should a man refuse mercy to his fellows, yet seek pardon for his own sins?
28.5: If he who is but flesh cherishes wrath, who will forgive his sins?
28.6: Remember your last days, set enmity aside; remember death and decay, and cease from sin!
28.7: Think of the commandments, hate not your neighbor; of the Most High's covenant, and overlook faults.
28.8: Avoid strife and your sins will be fewer, for a quarrelsome man kindles disputes,
28.9: Commits the sin of disrupting friendship and sows discord among those at peace.
28.10: The more wood, the greater the fire, the more underlying it, the fiercer the fight; The greater a man's strength, the sterner his anger, the greater his power, the greater his wrath.
28.11: Pitch and resin make fires flare up, and insistent quarrels provoke bloodshed.
28.12: If you blow upon a spark, it quickens into flame, if you spit on it, it dies out; yet both you do with your mouth!
28.13: Cursed be gossips and the double-tongued, for they destroy the peace of many.
28.14: A meddlesome tongue subverts many, and makes them refugees among the peoples; It destroys walled cities, and overthrows powerful dynasties.
28.15: A meddlesome tongue can drive virtuous women from their homes and rob them of the fruit of their toil;
28.16: Whoever heeds it has no rest, nor can he dwell in peace.
28.17: A blow from a whip raises a welt, but a blow from the tongue smashes bones;
28.18: Many have fallen by the edge of the sword, but not as many as by the tongue.
28.19: Happy he who is sheltered from it, and has not endured its wrath; Who has not borne its yoke nor been fettered with its chain;
28.20: For its yoke is a yoke of iron and its chains are chains of bronze!
28.21: Dire is the death it inflicts, besides which even the nether world is a gain;
28.22: It will not take hold among the just nor scorch them in its flame,
28.23: But those who forsake the LORD will fall victims to it, as it burns among them unquenchably! It will hurl itself against them like a lion; like a panther, it will tear them to pieces.
28.24: As you hedge round your vineyard with thorns, set barred doors over your mouth;
28.25: As you seal up your silver and gold, so balance and weigh your words.
28.26: Take care not to slip by your tongue and fall victim to your foe waiting in ambush.

29.1: He does a kindness who lends to his neighbor, and he fulfills the precepts who holds out a helping hand.
29.2: Lend to your neighbor in his hour of need, and pay back your neighbor when a loan falls due;
29.3: Keep your promise, be honest with him, and you will always come by what you need.
29.4: Many a man who asks for a loan adds to the burdens of those who help him;
29.5: When he borrows, he kisses the lender's hand and speaks with respect of his creditor's wealth; But when payment is due he disappoints him and says he is helpless to meet the claim.
29.6: If the lender is able to recover barely half, he considers this an achievement; If not, he is cheated of his wealth and acquires an enemy at no extra charge; With curses and insults the borrower pays him back, with abuse instead of honor.
29.7: Many refuse to lend, not out of meanness, but from fear of being cheated.
29.8: To a poor man, however, be generous; keep him not waiting for your alms;
29.9: Because of the precept, help the needy, and in their want, do not send them away empty-handed.
29.10: Spend your money for your brother and friend, and hide it not under a stone to perish;
29.11: Dispose of your treasure as the Most High commands, for that will profit you more than the gold.
29.12: Store up almsgiving in your treasure house, and it will save you from every evil;
29.13: Better than a stout shield and a sturdy spear it will fight for you against the foe.
29.14: A good man goes surety for his neighbor, and only the shameless would play him false;
29.15: Forget not the kindness of your backer, for he offers his very life for you.
29.16: The wicked turn a pledge on their behalf into misfortune, and the ingrate abandons his protector;
29.17: Going surety has ruined many prosperous men and tossed them about like waves of the sea,
29.18: Has exiled men of prominence and sent them wandering through foreign lands.
29.19: The sinner through surety comes to grief, and he who undertakes too much falls into lawsuits.
29.20: Go surety for your neighbor according to your means, but take care lest you fall thereby.
29.21: Life's prime needs are water, bread, and clothing, a house, too, for decent privacy.
29.22: Better a poor man's fare under the shadow of one's own roof than sumptuous banquets among strangers.
29.23: Be it little or much, be content with what you have, and pay no heed to him who would disparage your home;
29.24: A miserable life it is to go from house to house, for as a guest you dare not open your mouth.
29.25: The visitor has no thanks for filling the cups; besides, you will hear these bitter words:
29.26: "Come here, stranger, set the table, give me to eat the food you have!
29.27: Away, stranger, for one more worthy; for my brother's visit I need the room!"
29.28: Painful things to a sensitive man are abuse at home and insults from his creditors.

30.1: He who loves his son chastises him often, that he may be his joy when he grows up.
30.2: He who disciplines his son will benefit from him, and boast of him among his intimates.
30.3: He who educates his son makes his enemy jealous, and shows his delight in him among his friends.
30.4: At the father's death, he will seem not dead, since he leaves after him one like himself,
30.5: Whom he looks upon through life with joy, and even in death, without regret:
30.6: The avenger he leaves against his foes, and the one to repay his friends with kindness.
30.7: He who spoils his son will have wounds to bandage, and will quake inwardly at every outcry.
30.8: A colt untamed turns out stubborn; a son left to himself grows up unruly.
30.9: Pamper your child and he will be a terror for you, indulge him and he will bring you grief.
30.10: Share not in his frivolity lest you share in his sorrow, when finally your teeth are clenched in remorse.
30.11: Give him not his own way in his youth, and close not your eyes to his follies.
30.12: Bend him to the yoke when he is young, thrash his sides while he is still small, lest he become stubborn, disobey you, and leave you disconsolate.
30.13: Discipline your son, make heavy his yoke, lest his folly humiliate you.
30.14: Better a poor man strong and robust, than a rich man with wasted frame.
30.15: More precious than gold is health and well-being, contentment of spirit than coral.
30.16: No treasure greater than a healthy body; no happiness, than a joyful heart!
30.17: Preferable is death to a bitter life, unending sleep to constant illness.
30.18: Dainties set before one who cannot eat are like the offerings placed before a tomb.
30.19: What good is an offering to an idol that can neither taste nor smell?
30.20: So it is with the afflicted man who groans at the good things his eyes behold!
30.21: Do not give in to sadness, torment not yourself with brooding;
30.22: Gladness of heart is the very life of man, cheerfulness prolongs his days.
30.23: Distract yourself, renew your courage, drive resentment far away from you; for worry has brought death to many, nor is there aught to be gained from resentment.
30.24: Envy and anger shorten one's life, worry brings on premature old age.
30.25: One who is cheerful and gay while at table benefits from his food.

31.1: Keeping watch over riches wastes the flesh, and the care of wealth drives away rest.
31.2: Concern for one's livelihood banishes slumber; more than a serious illness it disturbs repose.
31.3: The rich man labors to pile up wealth, and his only rest is wanton pleasure;
31.4: The poor man toils for a meager subsistence, and if ever he rests, he finds himself in want.
31.5: The lover of gold will not be free from sin, for he who pursues wealth is led astray by it.
31.6: Many have been ensnared by gold, though destruction lay before their eyes;
31.7: It is a stumbling block to those who are avid for it, a snare for every fool.
31.8: Happy the rich man found without fault, who turns not aside after gain!
31.9: Who is he, that we may praise him? he, of all his kindred, has done wonders,
31.10: For he has been tested by gold and come off safe, and this remains his glory; He could have sinned but did not, could have done evil but would not,
31.11: So that his possessions are secure, and the assembly recounts his praises.
31.12: If you are dining with a great man, bring not a greedy gullet to his table, Nor cry out, "How much food there is here!"
31.13: Remember that gluttony is evil. No creature is greedier than the eye: therefore it weeps for any cause.
31.14: Recognize that your neighbor feels as you do, and keep in mind your own dislikes:
31.15: Toward what he eyes, do not put out a hand; nor reach when he does for the same dish.
31.16: Behave at table like a favored guest, and be not greedy, lest you be despised.
31.17: Be the first to stop, as befits good manners; gorge not yourself, lest you give offense.
31.18: If there are many with you at table, be not the first to reach out your hand.
31.19: Does not a little suffice for a well-bred man? When he lies down, it is without discomfort.
31.20: Distress and anguish and loss of sleep, and restless tossing for the glutton! Moderate eating ensures sound slumber and a clear mind next day on rising.
31.21: If perforce you have eaten too much, once you have emptied your stomach, you will have relief.
31.22: Listen to me, my son, and scorn me not; later you will find my advice good. In whatever you do, be moderate, and no sickness will befall you.
31.23: On a man generous with food, blessings are invoked, and this testimony to his goodness is lasting;
31.24: He who is miserly with food is denounced in public, and this testimony to his stinginess is lasting.
31.25: Let not wine-drinking be the proof of your strength, for wine has been the ruin of many.
31.26: As the furnace probes the work of the smith, so does wine the hearts of the insolent.
31.27: Wine is very life to man if taken in moderation. Does he really live who lacks the wine which was created for his joy?
31.28: Joy of heart, good cheer and merriment are wine drunk freely at the proper time.
31.29: Headache, bitterness and disgrace is wine drunk amid anger and strife.
31.30: More and more wine is a snare for the fool; it lessens his strength and multiplies his wounds.
31.31: Rebuke not your neighbor when wine is served, nor put him to shame while he is merry; Use no harsh words with him and distress him not in the presence of others.

32.1: If you are chosen to preside at dinner, be not puffed up, but with the guests be as one of themselves; Take care of them first before you sit down;
32.2: when you have fulfilled your duty, then take your place, to share in their joy and win praise for your hospitality.
32.3: Being older, you may talk; that is only your right, but temper your wisdom, not to disturb the singing.
32.4: When wine is present, do not pour out discourse, and flaunt not your wisdom at the wrong time.
32.5: Like a seal of carnelian in a setting of gold is a concert when wine is served.
32.6: Like a gold mounting with an emerald seal is string music with delicious wine.
